comparison_method: intersect Paper 3 holdout IDs with Paper 2 per-id refusal judgments
selection_rule: rank by sep*|cos|; restrict to depth band [0.55, 0.80]; take top-k

Qwen/Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct.note: loss flat; mid-depth selection is 3x weaker than top-of-net

meta-llama/Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct.note: loss flat; mid-depth selection is 6x weaker than top-of-net (depth band could not yield 4 contiguous high-score blocks)

google/gemma-3-4b-it.note: mid-depth and top-of-net produced the SAME selection (Gemma's top-of-net was already inside the mid-depth band); this run is effectively a re-evaluation of the e=6 top-of-net adapter at temperature 1

headline_finding: The probe-depth hypothesis is rejected. Mid-depth selection did not unlock training on Qwen or Llama (loss still flat, margins still <0.05 in both cases). Gemma's mid-depth selection was identical to its top-of-net selection (it was already at depth 0.71-0.79). The training asymmetry across anchors is not explained by where the probe selected blocks; it is a property of the anchor itself (architecture / tokenizer / attention impl / base alignment).
next_move_options: F: LR sweep on Qwen and Llama (1e-5, 2e-5, 5e-5). Cheapest binary test of 'are these anchors trainable at all on this data?'. ~36 GPU-min, $0 OpenRouter.
G: Gemma at +500 pairs. Tests whether OOD generalisation lifts with more data on the one anchor we know trains. ~$5 OpenRouter, ~10 GPU-min.
